source.title: Bayesian optimization with evolutionary and structure-based regularization for directed protein evolution

initial_request.research_problem: Directed evolution optimizes a target property but may degrade unmeasured properties (e.g., solubility, thermostability). ML-assisted DE models are myopic, exacerbating this trade-off.
initial_request.target_object: Protein variants with improved target property while maintaining unmeasured native-like properties
initial_request.known_context: Directed evolution is under-determined; mutations for target property may harm unmeasured properties.
input_data: BRCA1 E3 ligase activity data (1388 variants)
Spike ACE2 binding affinity data (4020 variants)
quantifiable_goals: Improve target property over traditional DE

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Directed evolution optimization is under-determined; mutations for target property may harm unmeasured properties.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to bias ML-assisted DE toward designs that maintain unmeasured native-like properties?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Regularizing Bayesian optimization with evolutionary or structure-based constraints will yield better designs by maintaining unmeasured native-like properties.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Formulate DE as a regularized Bayesian optimization problem.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Regularization terms reflecting evolutionary or structure-based constraints can be incorporated into BO.

evidence.quote_or_span: the underlying optimization problem is under-determined, and so mutations introduced to improve the specified property may come at the expense of unmeasured, but nevertheless important properties (ex. solubility, thermostability, etc)
We address this issue by formulating DE as a regularized Bayesian optimization problem where the regularization term reflects evolutionary or structure-based constraints.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis formulated; need to implement regularization.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to incorporate evolutionary and structure-based constraints into BO acquisition?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Multiplying standard acquisition functions (EI, PI, UCB) by a regularization factor (evolutionary log-odds or structure-based ΔΔG) will bias selection.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Implement evolutionary and structure-based regularization components.

evidence.quote_or_span: By summing over these logits for a given variant sequence, we obtain a TPLM-derived log-odds score.
Calculate the energy associated with introducing each mutation into the structure using FoldX's BuildModel command. Calculations for each mutated sequence are done three times.
we use the squared exponential kernel
We used a one-hot encoding of the variants.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Regularized acquisition functions designed; need datasets and regularization implementations.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which datasets and regularization models to use?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: GB1, BRCA1, and Spike datasets cover diverse protein engineering scenarios; ESM-1b TPLM and FoldX provide evolutionary and structure-based regularization.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Select datasets and implement regularization models.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Datasets selected; evolutionary regularization via ESM-1b TPLM (and fold-family models) and structure-based regularization via FoldX implemented.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Evaluate baseline ML-assisted DE vs traditional DE on GB1.

evidence.quote_or_span: Experimental measurements were obtained for 149,361 out of 160,000 (i.e. $20^{4}$ ) possible variants at these four loci using mRNA display
The range of fitness scores is from 0 to 8.76, with mean 0.08
Our experiments were limited to the mutations in residues 2-103 that passed the quality filter, which correspond to the RING domain coordinates. In total, this gave us 1388 variant sequences.
Starr et al. [31] performed a deep mutational scan of the Spike glycoprotein using a yeast display platform to assess the effect of mutations in the receptor binding domain on ACE2 binding affinity.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does ML-assisted DE outperform traditional DE on GB1?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: ML-assisted DE will achieve higher fitness than simulated traditional DE (single mutation walk, recombination).
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compare unregularized BO against simulated traditional DE on GB1.
evolution_trajectory.observation: ML-assisted DE achieves 45% higher fitness (7.22-7.27 vs 4.97).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Evaluate regularized BO methods on all three proteins.

evidence.quote_or_span: ML-assisted DE outperforms simulated traditional approaches (i.e., single-mutant walk and recombination) when optimizing GB1 with respect to fitness.
ML-assisted DE thus thus improves upon traditional approaches in designing high fitness GB1 variants by an average of 45%.
27 (PI), and 7.08 (UCB), whereas simulated traditional approaches identify a variant with fitness 4.
08 (UCB), whereas simulated traditional approaches identify a variant with fitness 4.97.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do regularized BO methods improve designs over unregularized BO?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Structure-based regularization will usually improve designs; evolutionary-based regularization may be unreliable.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Evaluate regularized BO (evolutionary and structure-based) on GB1, BRCA1, Spike.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Structure-based regularization usually leads to better designs and almost never hurts; evolutionary-based regularization via TPLM is unreliable.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze failure of evolutionary regularization on BRCA1 and Spike.

evidence.quote_or_span: structure-based regularization usually leads to better designs, and almost never hurts.
evolutionary-based regularization via the as-is ESM-1b TPLM is unreliable.
we find that evolutionary-based regularization via the as-is ESM-1b TPLM is unreliable.
structure-based regularization usually produces better designs, and only did harm in one configuration (GP + UCB + FoldX on GB1)

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Evolutionary regularization failed on BRCA1 and Spike.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Why does evolutionary regularization via TPLM fail on BRCA1 and Spike?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: TPLM may not capture fold-specific constraints well for these proteins.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze performance of TPLM and compare with fold-family models.
evolution_trajectory.observation: TPLM underperforms for BRCA1 and Spike; fold-family models (HMM, MRF) do not perform much differently than unregularized methods.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Investigate why structure-based regularization is less effective for GB1.

evidence.quote_or_span: The results using an evolutionary-based regularization are mixed; it leads to better designs for GB1, but worse designs for BRCA1.
evolutionary-based regularization via the as-is ESM-1b TPLM is unreliable.
we find that evolutionary-based regularization via the as-is ESM-1b TPLM is unreliable.
it does very well for GB1—outperforming both structure-based and the unregularized methods, but it underperforms for BRCA1 and Spike.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Structure-based regularization less effective for GB1.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Why is FoldX regularization less helpful for GB1?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: GB1 structure lacks the binding partner, making FoldX ΔΔG less informative.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compare structural contexts of GB1, BRCA1, Spike.
evolution_trajectory.observation: GB1 structure did not include the binding partner; BRCA1 and Spike structures include binding partners, making FoldX more informative.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze how regularization affects exploration patterns.

evidence.quote_or_span: GB1 was the one protein where the structure did not include the binding partner
The structures used for the experiments on BRCA1 and Spike, in contrast, include the binding partner

evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Regularization concentrates exploration at select residues, increasing site-specific entropy.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze residue-specific entropy of variant selections.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Regularized methods show higher residue-specific entropy, indicating concentrated exploration at select residues.

evidence.quote_or_span: the inclusion of a regularization term will bias variant selection towards designs that score favorably according to the regularization criteria
the objective and regularization values carry some information about unmeasured traits

regularized methods for each protein have darker shading than their unregularized counterpart. This indicates that regularized methods explore more variant types at specific positions in each protein.
The regularized version tends to concentrate exploration at select residues, whereas unregularized methods select positions more uniformly

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does the Bayesian approach reduce experimental burden?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Bayesian optimization requires fewer acquisitions than prior non-Bayesian ML-assisted DE.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compare number of acquisitions to achieve high fitness on GB1.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Bayesian approach required 191 acquisitions vs 570 in prior work, a 67% reduction.

evidence.quote_or_span: we show that our approach reduces the wet-lab burden to identify optimal GB1 designs by 67%, relative to the results presented in [4] on the same data.
a Bayesian approach only required 191 fitness value acquisitions (20 initial observations plus 9 batches of 19) to identify designs with high fitness values
the experiment in [4] required 470 initial observations plus a single batch of 100 to find similarly fit designs
This is a 67% reduction in the number of sequences tested

validation_methods: Comparison of acquisition counts with prior non-Bayesian ML-assisted DE on GB1
metrics: Reduction in wet-lab burden (67% fewer sequences tested)
success_verification.final_conclusion: Structure-based regularization usually improves designs and almost never hurts; evolutionary-based regularization via TPLM is unreliable. Bayesian approach reduces wet-lab burden by 67% on GB1.
limitations: BRCA1 and Spike datasets only include single-site mutations, limiting traditional DE simulation to GB1.
FoldX predictions may be less accurate without complete structural context (e.g., missing binding partner for GB1).
